did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning-based Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically VGG16 and InceptionV3, to classify skin lesions and detect melanoma. The study employed transfer learning on dermoscopic image datasets (such as ISIC) to differentiate between malignant and benign lesions, achieving a classification accuracy of 91.5% and an F1-score of 92.1% with InceptionV3.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a study conducted at the 2025 International Conference on Automation and Computation (AUTOCOM). It details the implementation and evaluation of deep learning models (VGG16 and InceptionV3) using transfer learning on dermoscopic image datasets to classify melanoma. The authors report specific performance metrics (accuracy, F1-score, ROC-AUC) and compare their proposed methodology against existing algorithms, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Early Detection of Melanoma through Deep Learning-Based Skin Lesion Classification Using VGG16 and InceptionV3
journal: 2025 International Conference on Automation and Computation (AUTOCOM)
brief_summary: This study investigates the use of deep learning Convolutional Neural Networks, specifically VGG16 and InceptionV3, for the early detection and classification of melanoma from skin lesions. Utilizing transfer learning on public dermoscopic image datasets such as ISIC, the researchers aimed to differentiate between malignant and benign lesions to assist dermatologists. The models were evaluated against other algorithms including ResNet50 and DenseNet121 using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC. The experimental results demonstrated that InceptionV3 outperformed the other models, achieving a classification accuracy of 91.5% and an F1-score of 92.1%. The study concludes that InceptionV3 is a viable tool for melanoma detection due to its ability to capture multi-scale features and minimize statistical errors.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary diagnostic target for early detection and classification. The study focuses on differentiating malignant melanoma from benign lesions.

Used as the negative class to differentiate from malignant melanoma. The paper notes that the dataset contains both malignant melanoma and benign lesions, with benign lesions often being the majority class requiring handling of class imbalance.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic categories: malignant melanoma vs. benign skin lesions
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'classification of diagnosing skin lesions' and 'decide whether skin lesions are benign or malignant'. The models (VGG16, InceptionV3) are trained to differentiate between malignant melanoma and benign lesions using transfer learning on dermoscopic images. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC.

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration) and other public melanoma datasets
datasets_used.description: Public dermoscopic image datasets containing labeled skin lesion images. The paper mentions obtaining a 'huge dataset' from ISIC and 'other public melanoma data set' to include both malignant melanoma and benign lesions.

use.training: Used for training VGG16 and InceptionV3 models via transfer learning (fine-tuning on melanoma-specific data after pre-training on ImageNet).
use.validation: Used for validation via stratified k-fold cross-validation.
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation to assess performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC).
use.other: Pre-training weights were sourced from ImageNet.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate model performance by dividing the dataset into k-splits, training on k-1 splits, and evaluating on the remaining split, repeated k times.

statistical_methods_used.method: ROC-AUC (Receiver Operating Characteristic - Area Under Curve)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantify model performance by taking into account the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity, serving as a metric for differentiating between positive (melanoma) and negative (benign) cases.

statistical_methods_used.method: Comparison of Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the proposed models (VGG16, InceptionV3) against existing algorithms (ResNet50, DenseNet121, SVM, Random Forest) and assess the ability to correctly classify malignant and benign lesions.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC) and compared against existing algorithms without statistical significance testing.

ISIC and other public melanoma datasets.notes: InceptionV3 outperformed all other models (VGG16, ResNet50, DenseNet121, SVM, Random Forest) in accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC. The study notes that InceptionV3 has the highest precision and recall, indicating better correct classification of melanoma lesions and identification of all cases. ResNet50 and DenseNet121 had lower recall values compared to InceptionV3. Training times for deep learning models were higher than traditional ML methods (SVM, Random Forest).

architecture_used.input_size: Scaled down to a specific size (exact dimensions not reported)

VGG16.description: Deep network with simple architecture, rich in hierarchical representation learning, designed for object recognition in ImageNet contest.

InceptionV3.description: Processes images at a multilayer scale, allowing the model to see the image at different resolutions; capable of capturing multiple scale features.

training_details.learning_rate: Scheduling system used to reduce overfitting (exact values not reported)
class_imbalance_handling: Class weighting (larger penalty for minority class/melanoma)

preprocessing_techniques: Scaling down images to a specific size
Transfer learning: loading weights from ImageNet
Fine-tuning the last few layers of the models

Applied to diversify images in the training set and aid the model in generalizing well to unseen images by manipulating the image under different lights.
Used to tackle class imbalance by assigning a larger penalty to the minority class (malignant melanoma) in the loss function.

conclusions: InceptionV3 is identified as a viable and superior tool for melanoma detection compared to VGG16, ResNet50, DenseNet121, SVM, and Random Forest, achieving the highest classification accuracy (91.5%), F1-score (92.1%), and ROC-AUC (0.945).

InceptionV3 demonstrated the best performance in precision and recall, indicating its ability to correctly classify melanoma lesions and identify all cases while minimizing both false positives and false negatives.
The InceptionV3 model's ability to capture multi-scale features makes it particularly effective for melanoma detection, as it can handle variations in lesion size and texture.

Deep learning models (VGG16 and InceptionV3) require more training time than traditional machine learning methods (SVM and Random Forest), but their superior diagnostic accuracy justifies their use in clinical practices.

The study confirms that deep learning models, specifically InceptionV3, can facilitate prompt diagnosis of melanoma, potentially improving patient outcomes by assisting dermatologists in early identification.

Future work should focus on improving model robustness through additional data augmentation techniques and incorporating multi-modal data (e.g., demographic data, pathologic images) to enhance generalization across different skin types and lesion characteristics.
Real-time application of these models in healthcare facilities requires further iteration, calibration, and collaboration with dermatologists to ensure seamless integration into diagnostic workflows.
important_remarks: The study relies on public dermoscopic datasets (ISIC) without reporting specific dataset sizes, class distribution counts, or detailed demographic information, limiting assessment of population bias.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; model comparisons are based solely on descriptive metrics (accuracy, F1-score, ROC-AUC).

The paper acknowledges class imbalance (fewer malignant than benign cases) and employs class weighting and SMOTE, but does not report the specific weights or oversampling ratios used, nor does it validate performance on the minority class separately in the results table.
External validation is absent; the model was only evaluated on the training/test splits of the public dataset, raising concerns about generalizability to unseen clinical populations.

The authors note that deep learning models require significantly more training time (e.g., 14 hours for InceptionV3) compared to traditional ML methods (e.g., 6 hours for Random Forest), which is a deployment concern for resource-constrained settings.
The paper mentions future work on real-time application and integration with dermatologists but does not provide evidence of clinical workflow integration or prospective validation.
Interpretability is addressed via heatmaps (Grad-CAM) in the proposed system design, but the current results section does not include visual explanations or error analysis of misclassified cases.

The study is a conference paper presented at AUTOCOM 2025, which may have less rigorous peer review compared to major journals, and the scope is limited to binary classification of melanoma vs. benign lesions.
